metadata.purpose: QA Documentation Validation - JIRA vs Confluence Alignment Test
metadata.conflict_scenarios: Multiple real-world documentation drift scenarios added

requirements.name: Login Validation

jira_specification.details: User login endpoint must return HTTP 200 on successful authentication and HTTP 401 for invalid credentials. Must validate username and password against secure database with proper error handling.
jira_specification.error_handling: Detailed error messages for invalid credentials

confluence_specification.details: Login validation requires HTTP 200 for valid credentials and HTTP 401 for invalid credentials. Error handling must be implemented for invalid username or password.

requirements.notes: Both JIRA and Confluence are in complete agreement on login validation requirements.

requirements.name: Password Policy Requirements

jira_specification.details: Password must meet minimum length requirement of 12 characters. Must include uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and special characters. Passwords must be hashed using bcrypt with salt.
jira_specification.complexity: Uppercase, lowercase, numbers, special characters required

confluence_specification.details: Password minimum length requirement is 8 characters. Must include letters and numbers. Standard encryption required.

requirements.notes: CRITICAL SECURITY MISMATCH: JIRA requires 12-character minimum while Confluence specifies 8 characters. JIRA requirement is more secure and should be enforced. This represents a significant security vulnerability if not resolved.
requirements.recommendation: Adopt JIRA's 12-character minimum requirement. Update Confluence documentation immediately.

requirements.name: API Response Performance

jira_specification.details: API endpoints must respond within 2 seconds under normal load conditions (defined as 100 concurrent users). Performance testing required for all critical endpoints. Response time SLA: P95 < 2000ms, P99 < 3000ms.

confluence_specification.details: Performance testing should be conducted for API endpoints. Response times should be optimized for user experience.

requirements.notes: JIRA provides detailed performance SLA (2 seconds, P95/P99 targets, 100 concurrent users), but Confluence only mentions 'performance testing' without specific metrics. Implementation team will need clarification on acceptance criteria.
requirements.recommendation: Update Confluence with specific performance SLA metrics from JIRA. Define clear acceptance criteria for load testing.

requirements.name: Session Timeout Configuration

jira_specification.details: User sessions must expire after 30 minutes of inactivity. System must display warning message at 25 minutes. User can extend session by 15 minutes with one click. Session data must be securely stored.

confluence_specification.details: Session timeout is configured to 30 minutes of inactivity. Users receive a warning notification at 25 minutes before session expires. Session extension capability available.

requirements.notes: Both sources align on session timeout (30 min), warning (25 min), and extension capability. Minor difference: JIRA specifies 15-minute extension duration which Confluence doesn't mention, but this is a reasonable implementation detail.

requirements.name: Data Encryption in Transit

jira_specification.details: All data transmission must use TLS 1.2 or higher. Certificate validation required. Must support ECDHE cipher suites for forward secrecy. Implement HSTS with max-age of 31536000 seconds.

confluence_specification.details: Data encryption in transit must be implemented. HTTPS should be used for all communications.

requirements.notes: Both require encryption in transit, but JIRA provides detailed specifications (TLS 1.2+, ECDHE, HSTS, certificate validation) while Confluence only mentions 'HTTPS'. Implementation needs to follow JIRA's detailed requirements.
requirements.recommendation: Update Confluence with specific TLS version, cipher suite, and HSTS requirements from JIRA. Add certificate validation requirements.

requirements.name: Database Connection Pooling

jira_specification.details: Database connection pool size must be set to MINIMUM 20 connections. Maximum pool size should be 100 connections. Connection timeout: 30 seconds. Idle timeout: 900 seconds (15 minutes). Pool must use HikariCP driver.

confluence_specification.details: Database connection pool size should be set to 10 connections minimum. Maximum pool size should be 50 connections. Standard connection timeout recommended. Uses C3P0 connection pooling library.

requirements.notes: CRITICAL MISMATCH: JIRA was recently updated (2026-02-04) with new requirements but Confluence has NOT been updated. JIRA now requires HikariCP (modern, faster) while Confluence still references C3P0 (deprecated). Pool sizes conflict: JIRA min=20/max=100 vs Confluence min=10/max=50. This will cause production issues if Confluence specs are followed.
requirements.recommendation: URGENT: Update Confluence to reflect JIRA changes. Migrate from C3P0 to HikariCP. Update pool sizes to min=20, max=100. Add connection timeout specification.

requirements.name: Application Logging Configuration

jira_specification.details: All application logs must be sent to file system with rotation every 24 hours. Log level: INFO for production. Log format must include timestamp, thread ID, and stack trace for errors. Logs retained for 30 days.

confluence_specification.details: All logs must be centralized to ELK stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana). Log level should be DEBUG for better visibility. Logs should be retained for 90 days for compliance. Real-time log streaming required.

requirements.notes: MAJOR ARCHITECTURAL CONFLICT: JIRA requires file-based logging while Confluence specifies ELK stack (cloud-based). Log level mismatch: JIRA=INFO vs Confluence=DEBUG. Retention mismatch: JIRA=30 days vs Confluence=90 days. This represents two completely different logging architectures. Confluence change appears recent and not communicated to JIRA owner.
requirements.recommendation: Urgent alignment meeting required. Decision needed: File-based or ELK stack? If ELK chosen, update JIRA. If file-based chosen, update Confluence. Clarify log level and retention requirements.

requirements.name: Unit Test Code Coverage

jira_specification.details: Minimum code coverage requirement is 75% for all new code. All critical paths must have 100% coverage. Coverage reports must be generated with each build. Tools: JaCoCo for Java, Istanbul for JavaScript.

confluence_specification.details: Code coverage should be maintained above 60%. Coverage reports are optional. Any coverage tool can be used.

requirements.notes: QUALITY STANDARD CONFLICT: JIRA enforces stricter 75% minimum coverage (vs Confluence 60%). JIRA requires 100% coverage for critical paths, Confluence doesn't mention this. JIRA mandates specific tools (JaCoCo, Istanbul), Confluence allows any tool. JIRA requires coverage reports with each build, Confluence makes them optional. These differences will impact code quality metrics.
requirements.recommendation: Adopt JIRA's stricter standards (75% minimum, 100% critical, specific tools). Update Confluence. Make coverage reports mandatory in CI/CD pipeline.

requirements.name: Production Deployment Process

jira_specification.details: Deployments must occur during maintenance window (2-4 AM UTC). Maximum 2 deployments per week. Rollback plan must be documented before deployment. Blue-green deployment strategy required.

confluence_specification.details: Deployments should follow continuous deployment model. Multiple deployments per day are acceptable. Canary deployment strategy is preferred for gradual rollout. Automatic rollback on failure.

requirements.notes: FUNDAMENTAL PROCESS CONFLICT: JIRA enforces conservative deployment (2x/week, scheduled window) while Confluence advocates aggressive deployment (continuous, any time). Deployment strategy differs: JIRA=Blue-green vs Confluence=Canary. This represents a fundamental disagreement on deployment philosophy. Confluence change on 2026-02-01 was not synchronized with JIRA.
requirements.recommendation: Executive decision required on deployment cadence. If continuous deployment chosen, update JIRA and implement canary deployment. If conservative approach chosen, enforce JIRA specs and update Confluence. Establish deployment governance.

requirements.name: API Rate Limiting

jira_specification.details: Rate limiting must be implemented at 1000 requests per minute per IP address. Burst limit: 100 requests per second. Response header must include X-RateLimit-Remaining. Exponential backoff required for clients.

confluence_specification.details: No rate limiting is currently required. API endpoints should be accessible without throttling restrictions. Future rate limiting may be implemented based on usage patterns.

requirements.notes: COMPLETE CONTRADICTION: JIRA mandates rate limiting (1000 req/min, 100 burst/sec) while Confluence explicitly states 'NO rate limiting required'. This is a critical security and performance conflict. If Confluence specs are followed, API will be vulnerable to DDoS attacks and resource exhaustion.
requirements.recommendation: IMMEDIATE ACTION: Implement JIRA's rate limiting requirements. Update Confluence to reflect rate limiting as mandatory. Add DDoS protection measures. Implement monitoring for rate limit violations.

summary.risk_assessment: HIGH - Multiple critical conflicts detected. Production deployment at risk if not resolved.
